questions.prompt: What are the four components that define an agent?
options: Model + tools + loop + state
Prompt + database + UI + API
Planner + executor + critic + memory bank
Embeddings + retrieval + generation + citations
questions.explanation: An agent is a model that directs its own control flow toward a goal, equipped with tools to act, run inside a loop, with state accumulating across iterations. Remove the self-directed loop and you have a workflow — predefined code paths over LLM calls.

questions.prompt: What distinguishes a workflow from an agent?
options: Workflows can't use LLMs
In a workflow, code defines the path through the steps; in an agent, the model decides the next step itself
Agents are always cheaper to run
Workflows can't call tools
questions.explanation: Prompt chaining, routing, parallelization, orchestrator-workers, and evaluator-optimizer are workflows: the control flow is yours. An agent hands the model the steering wheel. Most production systems are workflows — and should be.

questions.prompt: When is an agent (rather than a workflow) the right choice?
options: Whenever the task involves more than one LLM call
When the step sequence can't be known in advance — if you can draw the flowchart, you should code the flowchart
When latency matters more than accuracy
When you want deterministic, auditable behavior
questions.explanation: Agency buys flexibility for tasks whose steps are unknowable upfront, and costs you compounding errors, growing context cost, harder debugging, and larger blast radius. If the path is predictable, encode it as a workflow and keep the determinism.

questions.prompt: Which are real costs of agency the lesson calls out? (Select all that apply.)
options: Errors compound across many sequential steps
Per-iteration context grows, raising cost
Debugging gets harder than for fixed pipelines
Agents cannot use more than three tools
questions.explanation: Each self-directed step can go slightly wrong, and the errors multiply; the transcript grows every iteration and is re-read each time; and nondeterministic paths are harder to debug than flowcharts. Tool count is a design question, not a hard limit — hybrids (bounded agents inside workflows) are normal.

questions.prompt: What is the ReAct pattern?
options: Interleaving thought → action → observation, with native tool calling as its modern implementation
Reacting to user feedback after each response
Re-running failed actions automatically
A frontend framework for agent UIs
questions.explanation: ReAct alternates reasoning about what to do next, taking an action (tool call), and observing the result. Modern tool-calling APIs implement it natively. It's the default for exploratory work — with a known tendency to rabbit-hole without global structure.

questions.prompt: What is the main advantage of plan-then-execute over pure ReAct?
options: It never makes mistakes
It front-loads structure, enabling approval gates and parallel execution — though plans need revision on contact with reality
It uses fewer tokens in all cases
It removes the need for tools
questions.explanation: Producing a plan first gives you something to review (approval gates), parallelize, and track progress against. The weakness is staleness: plans meet reality, so production planners must re-plan when steps fail or reveal new information.

questions.prompt: When does reflection/Reflexion actually improve results?
options: Always — self-critique is universally beneficial
When verification is easier than generation, and the critic is anchored to tests, schemas, or rubrics rather than self-assessment
Only with models above 100B parameters
Only when the critic is the same model instance as the generator
questions.explanation: An unanchored 'is this good?' self-check mostly produces agreement. Reflection earns its cost when there's an objective signal — failing tests, schema validation, a rubric — that makes the critique informative. Verify-easier-than-generate is the test to apply.

questions.prompt: How should extended thinking be used in agent planning? (Select all that apply.)
options: Budget it per task rather than maxing it everywhere
Combine it with external patterns like plan-then-execute rather than replacing them
Use it as the sole replacement for all planning structure
Recognize it moves deliberation inside the model
questions.explanation: Extended thinking gives the model internal deliberation room, and thinking budgets should match task difficulty. But it complements rather than replaces external structure — approval gates and parallel execution still need an explicit plan outside the model's head.

questions.prompt: What is agent memory, fundamentally?
options: A vector database attached to the model
Deciding what the model sees each iteration — the context window is the only memory it actually attends to
The provider's session storage between API calls
The model's weights updated during the run
questions.explanation: Models are stateless between calls (Module 1); whatever 'memory' an agent has is whatever the harness places in the context window this iteration. Every memory technique — compaction, files, retrieval — is a strategy for choosing those tokens.

questions.prompt: What is the first memory intervention to try before heavier machinery?
options: Fine-tuning the model on the conversation
Trimming tool outputs at the source and pruning stale results from the transcript
Switching to a model with a larger context window
Storing every message in a vector database
questions.explanation: Most context bloat is self-inflicted: oversized tool outputs re-read every iteration. Truncating at the source and dropping stale results is cheap and often sufficient — reach for compaction and external memory only when hygiene isn't enough.

questions.prompt: Which statements about compaction (summarizing the transcript) are true? (Select all that apply.)
options: It trades fidelity for context headroom
The summary prompt determines what information survives
Rewriting the transcript invalidates prompt caches
It's lossless if the summary is long enough
questions.explanation: Compaction is lossy by design — details not captured by the summary are gone, so the summarization prompt is load-bearing. And because the prompt prefix changes, cached prefixes (Module 8) miss afterward. No summary length makes it lossless.

questions.prompt: What do external memory files add over compaction?
options: They make the model's weights persistent
State persists across compactions and sessions, readable by both agent and humans — but the agent needs explicit instructions about when to write
They eliminate context limits entirely
They're automatically maintained by the API
questions.explanation: A scratchpad or notes file survives transcript rewrites and session restarts, and humans can inspect it. The catch: models don't spontaneously maintain files — the harness must instruct when and what to record. Retrieval-backed memory extends the same idea across sessions using Module 4's machinery.

questions.prompt: What drives the control flow of the canonical agent loop?
options: A planning module that schedules each step
The response's stop_reason: 'tool_use' means execute-and-continue, 'end_turn' is the natural exit
A fixed number of steps configured upfront
The user approving each iteration
questions.explanation: The loop calls the model with tools; if stop_reason is tool_use it executes the requested tools, appends tool_result blocks, and repeats. When the model answers without requesting tools (end_turn), the task is done. Everything else in agent engineering is refinement around this.

questions.prompt: A tool throws an exception mid-run. What should the loop do?
options: Crash the run and surface the exception to the user
Return the error as an is_error tool_result so the model can retry differently, switch tools, or report the blocker
Silently skip the tool call and continue
Restart the entire loop from the first message
questions.explanation: Errors returned as tool results give the model a recovery opportunity — this single pattern accounts for a surprising share of agent robustness. Crashing wastes the run; silent skipping produces answers built on missing data.

questions.prompt: Which stop conditions should a production loop layer? (Select all that apply.)
options: A max-iterations cap
A token/cost budget for the run
No-progress detection (same tool, same args, repeatedly)
Trusting the model alone to always terminate
questions.explanation: A loop whose only exit is 'the model decides it's done' eventually spins. Iteration caps, cost budgets, timeouts, and repeated-call detection are independent guards — and hitting one should return the partial work and transcript, not vaporize it.

questions.prompt: How should approval gates be assigned to tools?
options: Every tool call requires human approval
By blast radius: reads auto-execute, reversible writes execute with logging, destructive or outward-facing actions pause and ask
No gates — the system prompt's rules are sufficient
Randomly sample 10% of calls for review
questions.explanation: Gating everything makes the agent useless; gating nothing makes it dangerous. Classify by consequence: searches auto-run, file creation logs, deletes/sends/deploys pause the loop for approval. The gate is just an if-statement before execute() — the model proposes, the harness disposes.

questions.prompt: What is the 'harness'?
options: The model's internal attention mechanism
Everything engineered around the model: system prompt, tools, context curation, permissions, sandboxing, and orchestration
The GPU cluster serving the model
A testing framework for prompts
questions.explanation: The same model behind two products performs wildly differently — the difference is the harness. You can't change the weights, but you own every harness lever, and harness changes move agent quality as much as model upgrades do.

questions.prompt: Why do few, sharp tools beat many overlapping ones?
options: Providers charge per registered tool
Every tool competes for the model's attention and selection accuracy — overlapping options degrade its choices
APIs reject requests with more than 20 tools
Fewer tools make the loop run fewer iterations
questions.explanation: Tool schemas enter the context, and the model must choose among them. Ten well-named tools with crisp when-to-use descriptions outperform forty vague ones. Tools should also sit at the right altitude — one model intention per call — with error messages designed to be read by the model.

questions.prompt: How do prompt rules, permissions, and sandboxes relate?
options: They're interchangeable — pick whichever is easiest
Layered defense: prompt rules are suggestions, permissions are policy, sandboxes are physics
Sandboxes replace the need for permissions
Permissions only matter for multi-agent systems
questions.explanation: A prompt rule can be ignored or injected around; a permission system enforces what the agent may do; a sandbox bounds what it can do even when confused or compromised. Defense in depth with least privilege as the default is the harness engineer's posture.

questions.prompt: What do subagents buy a harness? (Select all that apply.)
options: Context isolation — the parent receives a summary, not forty file reads
Parallelism across independent subtasks
Tightly scoped permissions (e.g., read-only research)
Free execution — subagent tokens aren't billed
questions.explanation: Subagents are fresh contexts with narrow tasks and restricted tools: they isolate context pollution, run in parallel, and can be permission-scoped. The costs are real, though — coordination overhead, information loss at the boundary, and every subagent token is paid for.

questions.prompt: What problem does MCP solve?
options: The N×M integration problem: instead of every AI app building every integration bespoke, servers are built once and any MCP client can use them
Slow token generation in long conversations
The context window limit
Model hallucination on factual questions
questions.explanation: Before MCP, N applications × M data sources meant every cell hand-built. MCP standardizes the protocol between AI applications (clients) and integrations (servers) — like LSP did for editors — collapsing the grid to N + M.

questions.prompt: Which MCP primitive is controlled by the model?
options: Resources — data the application loads into context
Prompts — templates the user invokes
Tools — functions the model decides to call
Transports — how client and server communicate
questions.explanation: Tools are model-controlled (the model chooses to call them), resources are application-controlled, and prompts are user-controlled. Tools are the workhorse — they're Module 3's tool calling, standardized over JSON-RPC.

questions.prompt: When does a stdio transport make sense versus HTTP?
options: stdio for shared multi-user deployments; HTTP for local tools
stdio for a local child-process server with credentials staying on your machine; streamable HTTP (+ OAuth) for shared remote servers
They're identical in capability and deployment
HTTP is deprecated in favor of stdio
questions.explanation: A local server runs as a child process speaking JSON-RPC over stdio — zero network setup, local credentials. Remote servers speak streamable HTTP with OAuth so one deployment serves many users. The model never sees any of this; MCP is harness plumbing.

questions.prompt: Which cautions apply when adopting MCP servers? (Select all that apply.)
options: Every connected server's tool schemas consume context and can degrade tool selection
A malicious or compromised server is a prompt-injection vector via its tool results
Server credentials should be scoped to least privilege
MCP servers are sandboxed by the protocol, so vetting is unnecessary
questions.explanation: Connect what the task needs, not everything you have — schemas cost context. And a server is code running with real credentials whose outputs are untrusted input to your model (Module 8): vet it like any dependency and keep approval gates on consequential actions. The protocol itself provides no sandbox.

questions.prompt: What is the dominant production multi-agent pattern?
options: Debate between independent agents with majority voting
Orchestrator–workers: a lead agent decomposes the task, spawns scoped workers, and integrates their distilled results
A ring topology where every agent talks to every other
Two identical agents checking each other's work
questions.explanation: Orchestrator–workers dominates because it captures the two real benefits — context isolation (the lead pays for summaries, not transcripts) and parallelism — with manageable coordination. Anthropic's research system is the canonical example: ~90% quality gain on research evals for ~15× tokens.

questions.prompt: What kind of task is multi-agent best suited for?
options: Tightly coupled edits to a shared codebase
Read-heavy, parallelizable work that exceeds one context window — broad research, large-corpus exploration
Any task involving more than three steps
Low-latency interactive chat
questions.explanation: Workers earn their cost through isolation and parallelism, which pay off when subtasks are independent and read-heavy. Write-heavy coupled work produces conflicts and contradictions — agents don't coordinate unless you build the coordination.

questions.prompt: Which are real multi-agent failure modes? (Select all that apply.)
options: A worker's subtly wrong finding becomes the orchestrator's confident premise
Information loss at boundaries — workers know only their brief and can't ask follow-ups
Write conflicts between agents editing the same artifacts
Workers automatically share each other's context windows
questions.explanation: Errors compound across agents and propagate through summaries that strip caveats; under-specified briefs are the #1 practical failure; and concurrent writers conflict. Workers do NOT share context — that isolation is the feature, and the cost.

questions.prompt: What is the recommended escalation ladder for architecture complexity?
options: Start with full multi-agent and simplify if it's too expensive
Single call → workflow → single agent → agent with subagents → multi-agent, earning each step with a measured failure of the previous one
Always use whichever architecture the newest paper proposes
Choose based on team size rather than task shape
questions.explanation: Each rung adds cost, latency, and failure modes. Climb only when evals (Module 7) show the simpler design failing — if one agent with a clean context can do it, that's also the cheapest option. Architecture diagrams aren't evidence.

questions.prompt: What single mechanical fact makes context management the central agent engineering problem?
options: Models stream output token by token
The model re-reads the entire growing transcript every iteration — every appended token is paid again on every later step
Tools run sequentially
System prompts are limited to 4KB
questions.explanation: The loop appends and re-sends. Oversized tool outputs, stale results, and unpruned history tax every remaining iteration — hence trimming, compaction, and subagents.

questions.prompt: You can draw the complete flowchart for a task. What does this module say to build?
options: An autonomous agent with extended thinking
A workflow — code the flowchart; agency is for step sequences unknowable in advance
A multi-agent committee
An MCP server
questions.explanation: Agency costs compounding errors, cost, and debuggability. If the path is predictable, predefined code paths over LLM calls keep determinism.

questions.prompt: Which are structural (not textual) safety layers in a harness? (Select all that apply.)
options: Permission systems with least-privilege tool grants
Sandboxes bounding filesystem/network access
Approval gates pausing destructive actions
A system prompt rule saying 'never delete files'
questions.explanation: Prompt rules are suggestions; permissions are policy; sandboxes are physics. The structural layers hold even when the model is confused or injected.

questions.prompt: A research task needs forty file reads whose details would drown the main agent's context. Which harness feature fits?
options: Raise max_tokens
A subagent: isolated context, narrow brief, read-only tools, returning only a distilled summary
Compaction after every read
A bigger model
questions.explanation: Subagents convert context pollution into a one-paragraph result, parallelize, and scope permissions — the building block of orchestrator–workers.

questions.prompt: Your agent gets a tools/list response from a newly connected MCP server. What should you have already considered?
options: Nothing — MCP servers are sandboxed by the protocol
That its schemas now consume context, its credentials should be least-privilege, and its tool results are untrusted input
That stdio transport encrypts traffic
That the model now knows MCP exists
questions.explanation: Servers are vetted dependencies with real credentials whose outputs can carry injected instructions. Connect selectively; keep approval gates regardless of which server requests an action.

questions.prompt: Anthropic's multi-agent research system improved research evals ~90% — at what cost, and what does that imply?
options: No cost — multi-agent is strictly better
~15× the tokens of a single chat turn — so climb the single-call → workflow → agent → multi-agent ladder only on measured evidence
Double latency only
It required fine-tuning
questions.explanation: Parallel isolated workers buy quality on read-heavy research at a steep token premium. Each architecture rung must be earned by a measured failure of the simpler one.
